overview: Shippan occupies a narrow peninsula extending south into Long Island Sound, making it one of Stamford's most geographically distinct neighborhoods. Shippan Avenue runs the length of the peninsula, connecting the inland residential streets to the exclusive waterfront enclave of Shippan Point at the tip. The Stamford Yacht Club and the Halloween Yacht Club anchor the shoreline, while Cummings Park and West Beach Park provide public waterfront access. Roughly 1,100 homes span the full spectrum from modest mid-century bungalows near the peninsula's base to multi-million-dollar estates commanding direct Sound frontage.

children.text: Shippan's geography shapes everything about the neighborhood. The peninsula is roughly two miles long and less than half a mile wide in most places, with Long Island Sound pressing in from both sides. That compression means water is never far away, no matter where you stand. At the northern base, homes sit on standard suburban lots, and the streetscape could belong to any Stamford neighborhood. Walk south along Shippan Avenue, and the character shifts block by block. The lots widen. The trees thin. Salt air replaces the scent of cut grass. By the time you reach Shippan Point, the peninsula narrows to a promontory ringed by private beaches, stone seawalls, and estates built to frame the horizon.

The Shippan Point Association, founded in 1902, has spent more than a century protecting the peninsula's character through tree-planting campaigns, police patrols, and neighborhood events. Two yacht clubs define the social waterfront. The Stamford Yacht Club, established in 1890 at the home of William Lottimer, hosts roughly 550 members and organizes the Vineyard Race every Labor Day weekend — one of New England's premier offshore events. The Halloween Yacht Club sits along Westcott Cove and draws a membership focused on one-design racing and junior sailing. Between the two clubs, the Woodway Beach Club offers seasonal swimming, kayaking, and paddle boarding from May through September.

Public waterfront access sets Shippan apart from many Connecticut shoreline neighborhoods. Cummings Park, east of the peninsula, spreads across a broad parcel with tennis courts, basketball courts, softball fields, a swimming beach, and a fishing pier extending into Westcott Cove. West Beach Park anchors the western flank with soccer fields, baseball diamonds, and a playground. Our Lady Star of the Sea, a Roman Catholic parish on Shippan Avenue, operates a parochial school that adds another layer of community anchoring. The combination of public parks, private clubs, and parish institutions creates a self-contained social infrastructure that most Stamford neighborhoods cannot match.

The real estate gradient along this peninsula is dramatic. Inland homes near the peninsula's northern base — colonials, Capes, and beach cottages from the mid-twentieth century — trade in the $700,000 to $1.2 million range. Move south past the yacht clubs, and waterfront colonials and contemporary estates begin at $2 million and climb past $5 million for direct Sound frontage with private docks. The most exclusive properties at Shippan Point, set on oversized parcels with panoramic water views, have recorded sales above $10 million. That price range within a single neighborhood, from starter-level to estate-level, is virtually unmatched elsewhere in Stamford.

highlights: The Stamford Yacht Club, founded in 1890, hosts the annual Vineyard Race and maintains roughly 550 active memberships on the peninsula's eastern shore
Cummings Park provides public beach swimming, a fishing pier into Westcott Cove, and courts for tennis, basketball, and softball across a waterfront campus
Shippan Point estates on direct Long Island Sound frontage command prices from $2 million to over $10 million, with private beaches and deep-water docks
West Beach Park and the Woodway Beach Club bookend the peninsula's western shoreline with both public and private recreational access
Our Lady Star of the Sea parish and school on Shippan Avenue has served as a community anchor for generations of Shippan families
housingCharacteristics:
Shippan's housing stock reflects the peninsula's geography — a north-to-south gradient from modest inland homes to waterfront estates. Near the base, mid-century colonials, Capes, and beach cottages occupy standard lots, many with original floor plans intact and others renovated to contemporary standards. These properties trade between $700,000 and $1.2 million depending on condition and lot size.

Farther south along Shippan Avenue, lot sizes increase and architectural ambition grows. Colonial Revivals from the 1930s and 1940s share blocks with newer contemporary builds designed to capture water views from upper floors. At the tip, Shippan Point properties sit on oversized parcels — some exceeding an acre — with stone seawalls, private beaches, and dock access. Styles range from classic shingled estates to modern glass-and-steel waterfront homes. Condominium developments along the peninsula's edges offer an entry point — the Shippan address and water proximity without the maintenance burden of a single-family estate.
marketNotes:
Shippan's market is segmented by proximity to water. The inland tier near the peninsula's base moves at a pace consistent with Stamford's broader market — spring and fall listing surges, competitive bidding on updated homes below $1 million, and steady demand from buyers trading up from Stamford condos or relocating from New York City.

The waterfront tier operates differently. Inventory is scarce because owners rarely sell, and when listings appear, they often circulate through private broker channels before hitting the public portals. Buyers in the $3 million to $10 million band tend to be patient and specific — they want direct Sound frontage, a functional dock, or both. Marketing these properties requires measured pricing and presentation that emphasizes the irreplaceable nature of the location. The peninsula's finite land supply means no new waterfront parcels will be created, which provides long-term insulation against oversupply.
locationAccess:
Shippan sits approximately three miles south of downtown Stamford, reachable via Shippan Avenue and Magee Avenue. The Stamford Transportation Center — serving Metro-North, Amtrak, and multiple bus routes — is a ten-minute drive north, providing commuters with express train service to Grand Central Terminal in under an hour.

Interstate 95 is accessible from Exit 8, roughly five minutes from the peninsula's northern base. The Merritt Parkway connects through Stamford's northern corridors for drivers headed inland. Stamford's downtown restaurants, Harbor Point retail, and the Stamford Town Center mall are all within a short drive. Greenwich Avenue is approximately fifteen minutes west via I-95.
seoTitle: Shippan, Stamford CT Real Estate & Homes for Sale
seoDescription: Explore Shippan homes in Stamford CT — waterfront estates, yacht clubs, and direct Long Island Sound access. Contact Matt Caiola to tour the peninsula.

faqs.question: What is the price range for homes in Shippan, Stamford CT?
faqs.answer: Shippan spans an unusually wide price range for a single Stamford neighborhood. Inland colonials and beach cottages near the peninsula's northern base start around $700,000 and reach approximately $1.2 million. Waterfront homes along the mid-peninsula begin near $2 million. At the tip, Shippan Point estates with direct Long Island Sound frontage, private docks, and panoramic water views trade between $3 million and well over $10 million depending on lot size and condition.

faqs.question: What waterfront amenities are available in Shippan?
faqs.answer: Shippan offers both private and public waterfront access. The Stamford Yacht Club and Halloween Yacht Club provide members with sailing, racing, and social programming. The Woodway Beach Club operates seasonally with swimming, kayaking, and paddle boarding. Cummings Park, open to all Stamford residents, features a swimming beach, a fishing pier extending into Westcott Cove, and athletic courts. West Beach Park adds soccer fields, baseball diamonds, and a playground on the peninsula's western shoreline.

faqs.question: How far is Shippan from downtown Stamford and the train station?
faqs.answer: Shippan is roughly three miles south of downtown Stamford. The Stamford Transportation Center, which serves Metro-North Railroad and Amtrak, is a ten-minute drive from the peninsula's northern base. Express Metro-North trains reach Grand Central Terminal in under an hour. Interstate 95 is accessible via Exit 8, approximately five minutes north, connecting Shippan to Greenwich, Norwalk, and the broader Fairfield County corridor.
